schools.summary: Gill: the law is given after the law of the day of atonement and the rites belonging to it.

Keil & Delitzsch: chapters 17–20 are summed up as 'Israel is not to walk in the way of the heathen and of the Canaanites, but in the ordinances of Jehovah,' all relating to holiness of life — beginning with holiness of food.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
the laws of holiness following the atonement
Israel to walk in the ordinances of Jehovah, not as the heathen

schools.summary: Gill: Aaron and his sons, now constituted priests, whose office it was to offer the sacrifices of the people, ordinary and extraordinary.
Keil & Delitzsch: the address is to Aaron, his sons, and all the children of Israel, the law binding every man of the house of Israel who slaughtered an animal.

themes: Speak unto Aaron, and unto his sons, and unto all the children of Israel
this is the thing which the LORD hath commanded
a law touching every household

schools.summary: Gill: the law binds every man of the house of Israel, whether high or low, rich or poor.
Ellicott: it refers to the three sacrificial quadrupeds intended for private use — evident from 'killeth' instead of 'sacrificeth' — which must not be slaughtered within or outside the camp.
Wesley: not for common use, for such beasts might be killed by any person or in any place, but for sacrifice; 'in the camp, or out of the camp,' that is, anywhere.

themes: that killeth an ox, or lamb, or goat
in the camp, or out of the camp
the clean beasts bound to the one altar

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD would have every sacrifice brought to the door of the tabernacle, near which stood the altar of burnt offering and the priests ready for the service.
Keil & Delitzsch: 'blood shall be imputed' means the slaughter was reckoned as the shedding of blood, or bloodguiltiness, and punished with extermination (cf. Gen. 17:14).

themes: bringeth it not unto the door... blood shall be imputed
he hath shed blood... cut off
the sacredness of life and worship guarded

schools.summary: Gill: before the tabernacle was erected they offered in the open field and on high places, which was then lawful, but now made unlawful (though sometimes dispensed with for prophets like Samuel, David, Elijah).
Ellicott: the reason these animals must be brought to the sanctuary and offered first as peace offerings is to prevent the Israelites sacrificing them to the demons in the open fields.

themes: bring their sacrifices, which they offer in the open field
offer them for peace offerings unto the LORD
a hedge against idolatry, worship bound to the one altar

schools.summary: Gill: the priest sprinkles the blood upon the altar of burnt offering at the door of the tabernacle.
Ellicott: the priest who caught the blood in a bowl throws it upon the walls of the altar of burnt offering, and burns the fat (cf. Lev. 3:3, 5).

Wesley: the verse gives the reason of the law — God's propriety in the blood and fat, with which God was well pleased and the people reconciled; the two named as the most eminent, though other parts were also reserved for God.

themes: the priest shall sprinkle the blood... burn the fat for a sweet savour
God's propriety in the blood and fat
in the blood is atonement, in the fat the choicest

schools.summary: Gill: 'devils' may mean idols in general, for if men worship not God and Christ they worship devils; the calves of Jeroboam and the golden calf are so called; Israel is not to go a whoring after them any more, this being a statute for ever.

sides.position: Idols generally; even gross devil-worship as among the nations
Field-devils / he-goat demons of the wilderness

themes: no more offer their sacrifices unto devils
after whom they have gone a whoring
weaning Israel from false worship to the LORD alone

schools.summary: Gill: the charge is to Aaron and his sons and the children of Israel; whoever offers a burnt offering or sacrifice.

Keil & Delitzsch: whoever offered a burnt or slain offering and did not bring it to the tabernacle was to be exterminated — a prohibition of sacrifice in any other place, as the fuller law of Deuteronomy 12 shows, to suppress sacrificing to other gods.

themes: whatsoever man... that offereth a burnt offering or sacrifice
Israelite or stranger alike
all sacrifice confined to the one sanctuary

schools.summary: Gill: bringing it to the door meant offering it in a public manner by one of the LORD's priests, so that the man did not take on himself to be a priest, nor offer to an idol.
Keil & Delitzsch: he who brought it not to the tabernacle to offer it to the LORD was to be cut off from among his people.

themes: bringeth it not unto the door... cut off
offered publicly by the priest
the one appointed way of approach to God

schools.summary: Gill: the law reaches whatever man of the house of Israel, of every age, sex, or condition.

Henry: this is the third giving of the law against eating blood (Lev. 3:17; 7:26), besides the Noahic precept (Gen. 9:4); it binds the stranger too, which was one reason blood was for a time forbidden the Gentile converts (Acts 15:29); the penalty — God setting his face against the eater — is very severe.

themes: that eateth any manner of blood... I will set my face against that soul
binding Israelite and stranger
the apostolic decree forbidding blood (Acts 15:29)

schools.summary: Gill: the animal life or soul is in the blood — the primary seat of life, the fountain of life, as Harvey (who discovered the circulation) said, the first that lives and last that dies; God forbids eating it because he has given it upon the altar to make atonement, for it is the blood that maketh atonement for the soul.

themes: the life of the flesh is in the blood
I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement
the blood of Christ the true atonement (Heb. 9:22)

schools.summary: Gill: the law, great or small, is repeated for the reason now given, which was the true ground of this law all along.

Ellicott: because blood is the vehicle of life and ordained by God to atone for life, the children of Israel are forbidden to eat it; the strangers too, having submitted to the law of the land, lest their eating lessen the horror with which it was regarded — hence the apostolic enforcement (Acts 15:20; 21:25).

themes: No soul of you shall eat blood
neither shall any stranger
the blood ordained to atone, not to be eaten

schools.summary: Gill: the form of speech, often used in this chapter, marks the persons on whom the law is binding — Israelites and proselytes of righteousness.

Ellicott: hitherto the law mainly discussed the blood of sacrificial animals or those slaughtered at home; here it is extended to all other clean creatures, though wild, taken in hunting, which the Hebrew pursued of necessity for food.

themes: which hunteth and catcheth any beast or fowl
pour out the blood... and cover it with dust
reverence for the life that belongs to God

schools.summary: Gill: it is the life of all flesh, of every animal.

Keil & Delitzsch: 'its blood makes out its soul' — the blood was the soul of the being living in the flesh; therefore it was not to be eaten in any animal, and even in creatures not fit for sacrifice was to run out upon the ground and be covered with earth; whosoever eateth it shall be cut off.

themes: it is the life of all flesh; the blood of it is for the life thereof
ye shall eat the blood of no manner of flesh
the blood as the life belongs to God

schools.summary: Gill: every soul that ate of a beast that died of itself, even the quantity of an olive, broke this law, such creatures having their blood not regularly let out; Pythagoras is thought to have taken from this his rule to abstain from all that died of itself.
Keil & Delitzsch: he who ate of that which died of itself or was torn, whether of his own country or a stranger, was to wash his clothes, bathe himself, and be unclean until the evening.

themes: that which died of itself, or that which was torn
wash his clothes, and bathe himself
the law of blood reaching even to carrion

schools.summary: Gill: if he washes not, his guilt remains on him, and he shall suffer the punishment the law exposes him to, either by the hand of God or the civil magistrate.
Ellicott: if he neglects these acts of purification and enters the sanctuary defiled or partakes of the sacrificial meal, he incurs excision for the former and stripes for the latter.

themes: if he wash them not... then he shall bear his iniquity
the guilt remaining upon him
provided cleansing must be used, or guilt remains
